text:
The ultrafine structural features of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) at the sub-angstrom or picometer level are crucial for improving the separation selectivity of the gases with similar diameters. However, for the MOFs with larger pore sizes, the effect of the ultrafine structural features on the separation performance would be greatly weakened. Herein, an integrative strategy of extreme pore compression and ultrafine structural modification is proposed to enhance the separation performance of MOFs. The *acs* (*pacs*) architecture in the partitioned *acs* (*pacs*) (P-*) platform, which is featured with multimodular structure and large number of isoreticular structures, is particularly beneficial for the integration of the two structural features.

The primary function of the first ligand, which we denote as Ligand 1 (L1), is to reduce the *pacs* pore size to the minimum possible value while preserving their integrity. By using *trans*-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate as a bioisostere of 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate in place of the conventional dicarboxylate, we can achieve an extreme compression ratio of 30% or more, reflected in a unit cell *c*/*a* ratio of less than 1.3. Then, the second ligand (denoted as Ligand 2 or L2) further fine tunes the pore structure at an ultrafine scale of less than 3% compression. The use of this dual-ligand strategy yields C₂H₂/CO₂ selectivity of up to 20.8, significantly improved by a factor of 8 or more and associated with the best experimental breakthrough performance. The use of *trans*-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate as the shortest cyclic dicarboxylate bioisostere to a para-benzene segment in the MOF structure, here reported for the first time, enables further opportunities for structural design optimization in MOFs.
